headline: JPMorgan’s US$8b EA Buyout Deal And What It Means For Valuation
summary: JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) is leading a consortium of banks in launching an $8b junk bond sale. The debt issuance is intended to finance the leveraged buyout of video game publisher Electronic Arts. The transaction highlights JPMorgan's central role in arranging large, higher risk financing for major deals. For you as an investor, this deal points to where JPMorgan's investment banking engine is currently focused: large, complex financings that rely on appetite for below investment grade...

headline: Japan reports lower-than-expected core inflation for February, headline CPI eases for a fourth month
summary: The consumer price index fell to 1.3% last month, its lowest level since March 2022 and below the central bank's 2% target. It was down from 1.5% in January.

headline: JPMorgan offers clients a new way to hedge AI debt risk
summary: (Bloomberg) -- JPMorgan Chase & Co. is offering clients a new way to bet against the debt of five hyperscalers, as investors seek more-liquid hedges amid an unprecedented borrowing spree to finance artificial-intelligence infrastructure.The bank last month launched a basket of credit default swaps in Alphabet Inc., Amazon.com Inc., Meta Platforms Inc., Microsoft Corp. and Oracle Corp., according to people familiar with the matter. Trades occur in $25 million increments — $5 million for each firm

headline: With Mortgage Rates at a 3-Month High, These Stocks Are Quietly Winning
summary: The 30-year fixed mortgage rate climbed to 6.22% as of March 19, 2026, a three-month high last seen in December of 2025, as the 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.3% amid broader market unease. Rates had bottomed at 5.98% in late February before climbing steadily through March. The spring housing market is feeling the pressure. ... headline: How The Gogo (GOGO) Narrative Is Shifting After Softer 2026 Guidance And Target Cuts
summary: Gogo’s analyst fair value estimate has been trimmed from about US$10.67 to roughly US$9.33 per share, a reduction of around 13% that resets expectations for what investors are willing to pay today. Both bullish and bearish analysts point to softer 2026 guidance and pressure on legacy revenue as reasons for this shift, even though they read the implications for execution risk and long term potential very differently. As you read on, you will see how this evolving narrative might shape the way...
